channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: The WHO hates how Sweden is saving lives
item.description: Sweden is presented as a real-world example of how smoking rates can fall when adults are given access to safer nicotine alternatives. Rather than trying to eliminate nicotine entirely, the Swedish approach focuses on moving people away from combustible cigarettes and towards smoke-free products such as snus and nicotine pouches. The World Health Organization’s warnings about these products are criticised as overly moralistic, especially when cigarettes remain widely available in many countries. Canada is used as a contrasting example, where smoke-free nicotine products are heavily restricted while cigarettes can still be bought easily. The piece argues that public health should focus less on fear of nicotine itself and more on reducing the harms caused by smoking. (Published on June 01, 2026byjunonews.com)

item.title: As EU Reviews Tobacco Rules, Experts Warn Against Overlooking Smokers’ Alternatives
item.description: Experts believe the EU should consider the role of e-cigarettes, heated tobacco, and nicotine pouches in helping smokers quit. They caution against overly strict regulations, emphasizing the importance of adult smoker preferences. The decline in smoking rates is credited to various factors, not just EU regulations. The evaluation focuses on absolute risk, but experts stress the need to compare alternatives to continued smoking. They advocate for proportional regulations, warning against blanket bans or low nicotine limits that could deter smokers from switching to safer options. Scientists and consumer input are vital in shaping future policies. Policymakers should balance youth protection with adult cessation needs and conduct transparent, inclusive evidence-based discussions. (Published on June 01, 2026by2firsts.com)

item.title: What's wrong with WHO? Ten fundamentals on tobacco and nicotine
item.description: Global tobacco policy has become increasingly disconnected from the main cause of tobacco-related disease: smoking itself. Safer nicotine products such as vapes, nicotine pouches and heated tobacco products are presented as part of the solution, yet many health authorities continue to treat them as a threat rather than an opportunity for harm reduction. Misleading statistics, particularly around youth vaping, can encourage policies that fail to reduce smoking and may unintentionally push people back towards cigarettes. Strong restrictions and bans risk expanding illicit markets while making safer alternatives harder for adults who smoke to access. A more effective approach would be evidence-based, risk-proportionate regulation that recognises the significant differences between combustible cigarettes and smoke-free nicotine products. (Published on June 01, 2026byclivebates.com)

item.title: PAPAIOANNOY: Safer nicotine products are not going away — Canada needs to face reality
item.description: Canada’s approach to safer nicotine products is criticised for relying on restrictions while smoking rates remain a serious public health concern. Sweden is presented as evidence that access to smoke-free alternatives can help drive smoking down towards historically low levels. Heavy restrictions on vaping and nicotine pouches risk pushing consumers towards illicit markets instead of encouraging them to switch away from cigarettes. A stronger harm reduction strategy would focus on the difference between combustible tobacco and lower-risk nicotine products. Canada is urged to accept that safer nicotine alternatives are not disappearing and to regulate them in a way that protects consumers while supporting smokers who want to quit. (Published on June 01, 2026bywesternstandard.news)

item.title: Study shows loved ones benefit when smokers switch to non-combustible alternatives
item.description: Switching from cigarettes to non-combustible nicotine alternatives is linked not only to benefits for smokers, but also to improvements for the people closest to them. Loved ones reported better quality of life, improved emotional wellbeing and more positive shared experiences when smokers moved to products such as vapes, heated tobacco or nicotine pouches. The findings suggest that harm reduction can have wider household and social benefits, including less exposure to smoke, fewer unpleasant smells and improved relationships. Support for adult access to safer nicotine alternatives was high across the countries surveyed, especially among those who had seen someone quit smoking with these products. Policies that treat vaping and smoking in the same way risk ignoring the very different risk profiles of these products and the real-life benefits reported by smokers and their families. (Published on June 01, 2026bybusinesstech.co.za)
